drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 24 hours on day 1

tier_1_regimens.notes: FLOT is a category 1 preferred perioperative regimen for resectable gastric or EGJ adenocarcinoma. It replaced the ECF regimen based on the FLOT4 trial. The regimen is given for 4 preoperative and 4 postoperative cycles. [1, 54]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 24 hours on day 1

tier_1_regimens.notes: FLOT + durvalumab is a category 1 preferred regimen for patients with PD-L1 CPS ≥1 or TAP ≥1%. In the MATTERHORN trial, this combination improved event-free survival (EFS) when added to FLOT. It is also an option for clinical node-negative tumors (category 2A), PD-L1 CPS <1 or TAP <1% (category 2B), and diffuse type (category 2B). [2, 55]
This is a category 1 preferred perioperative regimen for patients with good to moderate performance status. The dosing is based on the FOLFOX regimen used in clinical practice. [52]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 24 hours on days 1-5

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fluorouracil and cisplatin is a category 1 perioperative regimen. In the FNCLCC ACCORD 07 trial, perioperative chemotherapy significantly improved 5-year OS and DFS compared to surgery alone. [3, 52]

drugs.name: dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.schedule: over 30 minutes every 3 weeks for 3 cycles

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dostarlimab-gxly is recommended for neoadjuvant therapy only in patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. This is based on emerging data for neoadjuvant immunotherapy in this setting. [4]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Nivolumab and ipilimumab followed by nivolumab

drugs.name: ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: over 30 minutes every 3 weeks for 2 cycles

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is recommended for patients with MSI-H/dMMR gastric tumors in the neoadjuvant or perioperative setting. In the GERCOR NEONIPIGA trial, 29 patients underwent R0 resection and 59% had a pathologic CR. [5, 253]

Pembrolizumab is recommended for patients with MSI-H/dMMR gastric tumors in the neoadjuvant or perioperative setting. In a phase II trial, neoadjuvant pembrolizumab showed an ORR of 82% with a pathologic CR rate of 65%. [6, 254]
Tremelimumab and durvalumab is recommended for neoadjuvant therapy only in patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. In the INFINITY trial, the pathologic CR rate was 60%. [8, 9, 257]

Fluoropyrimidine-based chemoradiation is a category 1 preferred regimen for unresectable locally advanced disease if not previously received. In the INT-0116 trial, postoperative chemoradiation improved OS. [10-13, 58]

FOLFOX is a category 1 preferred regimen for unresectable locally advanced disease. In the PRODIGE5/ACCORD17 trial, FOLFOX-based chemoradiation showed similar PFS compared to fluorouracil and cisplatin. [16-18, 274]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 96 hours on days 1-4

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fluorouracil and cisplatin is a category 1 preferred regimen for unresectable locally advanced disease. [19-22]
Fluoropyrimidine and paclitaxel is a category 2B other recommended regimen for unresectable locally advanced disease. [23, 277]

drugs.name: trastuzumab
drugs.schedule: over 90 minutes initially, then 6 mg/kg every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-positive adenocarcinoma. In the ToGA trial, trastuzumab plus chemotherapy improved OS. [24, 146]

drugs.name: trastuzumab
drugs.schedule: over 90 minutes initially, then 6 mg/kg every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-positive and PD-L1 CPS ≥1 adenocarcinoma. In the KEYNOTE-811 trial, the addition of pembrolizumab improved PFS. [25, 26, 352, 353]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 96 hours on days 1-4

drugs.name: trastuzumab
drugs.schedule: over 90 minutes initially, then 6 mg/kg every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-positive adenocarcinoma. [27, 146]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 96 hours on days 1-4

drugs.name: trastuzumab
drugs.schedule: over 90 minutes initially, then 6 mg/kg every 3 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-positive and PD-L1 CPS ≥1 adenocarcinoma. [25, 26, 352, 353]

This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative and PD-L1 CPS ≥1 adenocarcinoma. In the CheckMate 649 trial, nivolumab plus chemotherapy improved OS and PFS. It is a category 1 preferred option when CPS ≥5. [28, 360, 361]

This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative and PD-L1 CPS ≥1 adenocarcinoma. In the KEYNOTE-859 trial, pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy improved OS and PFS. It is a category 1 preferred option when CPS ≥5. [29, 354]

This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative and PD-L1 CPS ≥1 adenocarcinoma. In the RATIONALE-305 trial, tislelizumab plus chemotherapy improved OS and PFS. It is a category 1 preferred option when CPS ≥5. [30, 171, 172]

drugs.name: zolbetuximab-clzb
drugs.schedule: over 1 hour on day 1 of cycle 1, then 1000 mg/m2 every 3 weeks

biomarkers.requirement: CLDN18.2-positive (≥75% of viable tumor cells with moderate to strong membranous staining)

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative and CLDN18.2-positive adenocarcinoma. In the SPOTLIGHT trial, zolbetuximab plus mFOLFOX6 improved OS and PFS. [31, 32, 173, 183]
FOLFOX is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative adenocarcinoma. [33, 34, 35, 279, 280, 281]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 96 hours on days 1-4

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative and PD-L1 CPS ≥1 adenocarcinoma. It is a category 1 preferred option when CPS ≥5. [29, 354]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 96 hours on days 1-4

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative and PD-L1 CPS ≥1 adenocarcinoma. It is a category 1 preferred option when CPS ≥5. [30, 171, 172]

drugs.name: fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion over 96 hours on days 1-4

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fluoropyrimidine and cisplatin is a category 1 preferred first-line option for patients with HER2-negative adenocarcinoma. [33, 36, 37, 38, 279, 282, 283, 284]

biomarkers.requirement: MSI-H or dMMR tumor (independent of PD-L1 status)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab monotherapy is a category 2A preferred first-line option for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors, independent of PD-L1 status. [39-42, 356, 357, 358]

drugs.name: dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.schedule: over 30 minutes every 3 weeks for 4 cycles, then 1000 mg every 6 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dostarlimab-gxly is a category 2A preferred first-line option for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. [43, 373, 374]

drugs.name: ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: over 30 minutes every 3 weeks for 4 cycles

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab and ipilimumab is a category 2A preferred first-line option for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. In the CheckMate 649 trial, this combination improved OS compared to chemotherapy. [28, 340]

biomarkers.requirement: MSI-H or dMMR tumor (independent of PD-L1 status)

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is a category 2A preferred first-line option for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors, independent of PD-L1 status. [28, 360, 361]

biomarkers.requirement: MSI-H or dMMR tumor (independent of PD-L1 status)

tier_1_regimens.notes: This combination is a category 2A preferred first-line option for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors, independent of PD-L1 status. [40, 41, 358]
FOLFIRI is a category 2A other recommended first-line regimen. In the FFCD 9803 trial, FOLFIRI was noninferior to ECF but with a more favorable safety profile. [44, 303]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Paclitaxel with or without carboplatin or cisplatin
tier_1_regimens.notes: Paclitaxel with carboplatin is a category 2A other recommended first-line regimen. [45-49]
Docetaxel with cisplatin is a category 2A other recommended first-line regimen. [50-53]
Fluoropyrimidine monotherapy is a category 2A other recommended first-line regimen. [37, 54, 55]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Docetaxel, cisplatin or oxaliplatin, and fluorouracil (DCF)
tier_1_regimens.notes: DCF is a category 2A other recommended first-line regimen. In the V325 trial, DCF improved OS and PFS compared to cisplatin and fluorouracil. However, it is associated with increased toxicities. [56-58, 304, 305]
Ramucirumab and paclitaxel is a category 1 preferred second-line regimen. In the RAINBOW trial, this combination improved OS, PFS, and ORR compared to paclitaxel alone. [59, 363]

Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is a category 1 preferred second-line regimen for patients with HER2-positive adenocarcinoma. In the DESTINY-Gastric01 trial, it improved OS, PFS, and ORR compared to physician's choice chemotherapy. [60, 61, 366, 367]
Docetaxel is a category 1 preferred second-line regimen. In the COUGAR-02 trial, docetaxel improved 12-month OS compared to active symptom control. [52, 53, 273]

drugs.name: paclitaxel
drugs.schedule: over 1 hour on days 1, 8, and 15 every 4 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Paclitaxel is a category 1 preferred second-line regimen. In the WJOG 4007 trial, paclitaxel and irinotecan showed similar OS. [48, 49, 62, 324]
Irinotecan is a category 1 preferred second-line regimen. In the AIO trial, irinotecan improved OS compared to best supportive care. [62-65, 271, 324, 325, 326]
FOLFIRI is a category 1 preferred second-line regimen if not previously used. [63, 66, 67]

drugs.name: trifluridine and tipiracil
drugs.schedule: twice daily on days 1-5 and 8-12 every 28 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: Trifluridine and tipiracil is a category 1 preferred regimen for third-line or subsequent therapy. In the TAGS trial, it improved OS and PFS compared to placebo. [68, 330, 331]
Ramucirumab monotherapy is a category 1 other recommended second-line regimen. In the REGARD trial, it improved OS compared to placebo. [69, 333]
Irinotecan and cisplatin is a category 2A other recommended second-line regimen. [34, 70]
FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab is a category 2A other recommended second-line regimen. [71, 72]
Irinotecan and ramucirumab is a category 2A other recommended second-line regimen. [73]
Docetaxel and irinotecan is a category 2B other recommended second-line regimen. [74]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy (Useful in Certain Circumstances)
tier_1_regimens.notes: These TRK inhibitors are category 2A options useful in certain circumstances for patients with NTRK gene fusion-positive tumors. Larotrectinib showed an ORR of 79% in a pooled analysis. [75-77, 343, 344, 345]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy (Useful in Certain Circumstances for MSI-H/dMMR)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is a category 2A option useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy in patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. In KEYNOTE-158, the ORR was 30.8%. [42, 78, 79, 80, 355]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy (Useful in Certain Circumstances for MSI-H/dMMR)

drugs.name: ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: over 30 minutes every 3 weeks for 4 cycles

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab and ipilimumab is a category 2A option useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy in patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. [28]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy (Useful in Certain Circumstances for TMB-H)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is a category 2A option useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy in patients with TMB-H tumors. In KEYNOTE-158, the ORR was 29%. [42, 81]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy (Useful in Certain Circumstances for MSI-H/dMMR)

drugs.name: dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.schedule: over 30 minutes every 3 weeks for 4 cycles, then 1000 mg every 6 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dostarlimab-gxly is a category 2A option useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy in patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. [43, 373, 374]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy (Useful in Certain Circumstances for BRAF V600E)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Dabrafenib and trametinib is a category 2A option useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy in patients with BRAF V600E-mutated tumors. In the NCI-MATCH trial, the ORR was 38% with a median PFS of 11.4 months. [82, 346]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy (Useful in Certain Circumstances for RET fusion-positive)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Selpercatinib is a category 2A option useful in certain circumstances for second-line or subsequent therapy in patients with RET gene fusion-positive tumors. In the LIBRETTO-001 trial, the ORR was 44%. [83, 347]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Gastric cancer is a malignancy arising from the stomach, with over 95% of cases classified as adenocarcinomas [1]. The stomach is anatomic organ located in the upper gastrointestinal tract with defined subsites including the cardia, fundus, body, antrum, and pylorus. Gastric cancer is typically classified based on anatomic location (cardia/proximal or noncardia/distal) and histologic type (diffuse or intestinal) [1,3,16]. The stomach wall consists of five layers: mucosa, submucosa, muscularis propria, subserosa, and serosa. Tumors may arise from the gastric mucosa and invade through successive layers, with depth of invasion determining T-stage [ST-1]. Tumors involving the esophagogastric junction (EGJ) are further classified using the Siewert system: Siewert Type I (adenocarcinoma of the lower esophagus with epicenter 1–5 cm above the anatomic EGJ), Siewert Type II (true carcinoma of the cardia at the EGJ, with epicenter within 1 cm above and 2 cm below the EGJ), and Siewert Type III (subcardial carcinoma with epicenter 2–5 cm below the EGJ, which infiltrates the EGJ and lower esophagus from below) [GAST-C 1 of 5]. Using the AJCC 8th edition staging, tumors involving the EGJ with an epicenter located >2 cm into the proximal stomach are staged as gastric carcinomas, while those with an epicenter ≤2 cm into the proximal stomach are staged as esophageal carcinomas [MS-4]. Gastric cancers located within the gastric cardia that do not involve the EGJ are staged as gastric carcinomas [MS-4]. The adjacent structures of the stomach include the spleen, transverse colon, liver, diaphragm, pancreas, abdominal wall, adrenal gland, kidney, small intestine, and retroperitoneum [ST-1]. Intramural extension to the duodenum or esophagus is not considered invasion of an adjacent structure but is classified using the depth of greatest invasion in any of these sites [ST-1]. The gastric lymphatic drainage includes perigastric lymph nodes (D1) along the lesser and greater curvature, and nodes along the named vessels of the celiac axis (D2) including the left gastric artery, common hepatic artery, celiac artery, and splenic artery [GAST-C 2 of 5].

subtypes.name: Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by variably differentiated tumor cells arranged in a tubular or glandular pattern with scattered goblet cells present. This type tends to form a mass lesion and is more prevalent in high-risk geographic areas. Intestinal type accounts for most of the geographic variation seen with gastric cancer [MS-2,1,3,16].
subtypes.frequency: More common than diffuse type overall; accounts for approximately 54% of gastric cancers in Western populations [MS-2,1].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with environmental factors including H. pylori infection, tobacco smoking, high salt intake, and dietary factors [MS-2,2,3,9,14-16]. More likely to overexpress HER2 compared to diffuse type [GAST-B 1 of 7,146]. HER2 positivity significantly higher in intestinal subtype (33%) versus diffuse/mixed histology (8%; P=.001) [139]. Associated with older age and distal stomach location [MS-14,116,119]. CLDN18.2 expression has been observed in intestinal-type tumors, though more commonly seen in diffuse subtype [175,176,178,180].

subtypes.name: Diffuse-type adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by poorly differentiated and discohesive tumor cells with a signet-ring or non-signet-ring morphology diffusely infiltrating the gastric wall in a desmoplastic stroma. More prevalent in low-risk areas and mostly associated with heritable genetic abnormalities [MS-2,3,9,14-16].
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 32% of gastric cancers in Western populations [MS-2,1].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with heritable genetic abnormalities including CDH1 germline mutations in hereditary diffuse gastric cancer [MS-2,1,3,9,14-16]. HER2 positivity significantly lower (6–8%) compared to intestinal type [139,140]. In the MATTERHORN trial, diffuse type gastric or EGJ adenocarcinoma showed no overall survival advantage when durvalumab was added to FLOT [GAST-F 3 of 9,d,*Tabernero J, et al. Annals of Oncology 2025;36:S1623-S1624]. Rising incidence observed in North America and Western Europe [MS-2,1,3,23]. Incidence rate per 100,000 in US Whites was 4.4 with male-to-female ratio of 1.9 [1].

subtypes.name: EBV-associated gastric cancer
subtypes.description: An estimated 8% to 10% of gastric cancers are associated with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection, making EBV-positive gastric cancer the largest group of EBV-associated malignancies [MS-16,185,186]. EBV-positive tumors occur preferentially in the proximal stomach and are associated with diffuse-type histology and early onset [MS-16,11].
subtypes.key_associations: Expression of PD-L1 is elevated in EBV-positive gastric cancers [MS-16,192-194]. An interferon-γ–driven gene signature was enriched in EBV-positive gastric cancers, suggesting increased sensitivity to PD-1/PD-L1 immunotherapies [MS-16,193]. Some studies suggest better overall survival rates compared to other genotypes [MS-16,187-191]. Prognostic value remains debated. EBV testing should be performed if morphology contains prominent lymphoid stroma [GAST-1,footnote e]. Not currently recommended for routine clinical care [MS-16].

subtypes.name: MSI-H/dMMR gastric cancer
subtypes.description: Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or mismatch repair deficient (dMMR) gastric cancers represent a distinct molecular subset with elevated mutation burden and favorable response to immune checkpoint inhibitors [MS-14,150-153,155-157].
subtypes.frequency: Incidence of 19% to 22% in gastric adenocarcinoma based on TCGA and pan-cancer sequencing data [MS-14,116].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with earlier stage, age ≥68 years, intestinal subtype, and distal stomach location [MS-14,116,119,150-153]. Better prognosis reported compared to microsatellite stable/MMR-proficient tumors [MS-14,150,151,155]. Associated with increased PD-L1 expression [MS-14,119,116,119,164,165,167,168]. Patients should be referred to genetics counselor for further assessment [GAST-B 5 of 7,13,160]. Respond to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in both neoadjuvant/perioperative and advanced/metastatic settings [GAST-F 3 of 9,GAST-F 4 of 9].

subtypes.name: HER2-overexpressing/amplified gastric cancer
subtypes.description: Gastric cancers overexpressing HER2 protein or harboring ERBB2 gene amplification. Unlike in breast cancer, the prognostic significance of HER2 status in gastric cancer is unclear [MS-12,131-140].
subtypes.frequency: Reported rates of 12% to 23% in gastric cancer [MS-13,132,133,139,140,142,143]. In the US population, HER2 positivity rate is 12% [MS-13,140]. In the ToGA trial: 32.2% in EGJ adenocarcinoma, 21.4% in gastric adenocarcinoma, 31.8% in intestinal gastric adenocarcinoma, and 6.1% in diffuse gastric adenocarcinoma [MS-13,145,146].
subtypes.key_associations: Higher in males versus females, EGJ tumors versus stomach tumors, and intestinal subtypes versus diffuse subtypes [MS-13,139]. Moderate to poorly differentiated grade more common [MS-13,133,137,139,140,142]. Treatment with trastuzumab-based regimens significantly improves survival [GAST-F 4 of 9,ToGA trial: median OS 13.8 vs 11 months; P=.046,146]. IHC scoring: 0 (negative), 1+ (negative), 2+ (equivocal requiring ISH/FISH), 3+ (positive) [GAST-B 3 of 7,147,148].

subtypes.name: CLDN18.2-positive gastric cancer
subtypes.description: Gastric cancers expressing claudin 18 isoform 2 (CLDN18.2), a tight junction transmembrane protein typically expressed in normal gastric epithelia and retained during malignant transformation [MS-15,173,174].
subtypes.frequency: 24% to 38% of advanced gastric and EGJ adenocarcinomas [MS-15,175-178]. Percentages vary across literature due to variations in detection methods, assays used, and differing definitions of positivity [MS-15,179].
subtypes.key_associations: More commonly seen in diffuse subtype than intestinal subtype [MS-15,175,176,178,180]. Independent of HER2 status, dMMR status, and PD-L1 [MS-15,176-178]. Positive defined as ≥75% viable tumor cells with moderate to strong membranous staining (2+ or 3+ intensity) by IHC [GAST-B 4 of 7,173,183]. Treatment with zolbetuximab plus chemotherapy significantly improves PFS and OS [SPOTLIGHT: HR for PFS 0.75, P=.0066; HR for OS 0.75, P=.0053,173; GLOW: HR for PFS 0.687, P=.0007; HR for OS 0.771, P=.0118,183].

subtypes.name: Genomically stable (GS) subtype
subtypes.description: A molecular subtype identified by The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) data, characterized by distinct genomic features from other subtypes [MS-11,116].
subtypes.frequency: Identified as one of four TCGA molecular subtypes [MS-11,116].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with diffuse histologic type [MS-11]. Characterized by genomic stability with limited chromosomal instability and MSI [MS-11,116].

subtypes.name: Chromosomally unstable (CIN) subtype
subtypes.description: A molecular subtype characterized by frequent somatic copy number alterations and gene amplifications, identified through TCGA and other molecular studies [MS-11,116,117].
subtypes.frequency: One of four TCGA molecular subtypes [MS-11,116].
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with intestinal histologic type and proximal location [MS-11,116]. Frequently harbors HER2 (ERBB2) amplification and TP53 mutations [MS-11,116]. Genome instability contributes to tumorigenesis through multiple pathways [MS-11,117,118].

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of gastric cancer involves an accumulation of genetic aberrations during gastric carcinogenesis, resulting in overexpression of growth factors and/or receptors, loss of tumor suppressor genes, alterations in the cell cycle, and changes in DNA repair and damage response [MS-11,116,117]. Genome instability, including increased chromosomal instability and microsatellite instability (MSI), is often associated with these changes [MS-11,117,118]. The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) identified four molecular subtypes of gastric adenocarcinoma [MS-11,116]. MSI-H occurs in approximately 22% of gastric adenocarcinomas based on TCGA data, and a pan-cancer analysis reported an incidence of 19% in MSI-H/dMMR gastric tumors [MS-14,116,154]. MSI-H tumors are associated with elevated mutation burden due to loss of functional mismatch repair (MMR) at microsatellite mutational hotspots [MS-14,118]. MMR deficiency is evaluated by IHC for nuclear expression of MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2 proteins [GAST-B 5 of 7,159]. MSI status is assessed by PCR using validated microsatellite markers including mononucleotide repeats BAT25, BAT26, MONO27, NR21, and NR24 [MS-14,158]. EBV-associated gastric cancer occurs in 8% to 10% of cases [MS-16,185,186]. HER2 (ERBB2) amplification and overexpression is observed in 12% to 23% of gastric cancers, with intestinal type more frequently positive [MS-12,132-144]. PD-L1 expression is frequently observed in intestinal subtype and is associated with MSI-H and EBV positivity [MS-14,164-168]. CLDN18.2, a tight junction protein, is retained during malignant transformation and represents a therapeutic target expressed in 24% to 38% of advanced gastric cancers [MS-15,173-178]. MSI-H/dMMR is frequently associated with increased immune checkpoint ligand expression [MS-11,119-121]. HER2 positivity also varies with histologic subtype: intestinal (33%) versus diffuse/mixed (8%; P=.001) [139]. Tumor mutational burden-high (TMB-H, ≥10 mutations/megabase) status serves as a biomarker for checkpoint inhibitor response [GAST-F 5 of 9,342]. The presence of NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions, RET gene fusions, and BRAF V600E mutations are additional molecular alterations with therapeutic implications [GAST-B 6 of 7]. Chromosomal instability in gastric cancer contributes to tumor development, progression, and therapy resistance through multiple signaling pathways [MS-11,117].

epidemiology.incidence: Globally, there were more than 968,000 new cases resulting in approximately 660,000 deaths in 2022, making gastric cancer the fifth most frequently diagnosed cancer and the fifth leading cause of cancer-related deaths in the world [MS-2,7,8]. The global incidence of gastric cancer shows wide geographic variation, with a 15- to 20-fold difference between high- and low-incidence regions [MS-2,1]. The highest gastric cancer incidence rates occur in Northeast Asia, South and Central America, and Eastern Europe [MS-2,5,6]. Rates are particularly high in Japan and Korea, where gastric cancer is the most commonly diagnosed cancer in males, and in China, where gastric cancer is a leading cause of cancer-related mortality [MS-2,5,6,9]. In the United States for 2025, an estimated 30,300 people will be diagnosed and 10,780 people are expected to die of this disease [MS-2,10]. Based on SEER data for 2024, gastric cancer is the 15th most commonly diagnosed cancer and the 15th leading cause of cancer-related death in the United States [MS-2,4].
epidemiology.mortality: Globally, approximately 660,000 deaths occurred in 2022, making gastric cancer the fifth leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide [MS-2,7,8]. In the United States for 2025, an estimated 10,780 people are expected to die of this disease [MS-2,10]. Gastric cancer generally carries a poor prognosis since it is often diagnosed at an advanced stage [MS-2]. In most countries where screening programs are not in use, approximately 50% of patients present with advanced disease at diagnosis and will likely have a poor outcome [MS-5].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of gastric cancer has decreased substantially in the United States and Western Europe over the past several decades [MS-2,1-4]. There has been a marked decline in intestinal type gastric cancers of the distal stomach in North American and Western European countries, mainly due to enhanced access to clean drinking water, improved food preservation, a diet with low promotion of gastric cancer, and H. pylori eradication [MS-2,1,3,16,23]. However, incidence rates of diffuse type gastric cancer of the proximal stomach are rising [MS-2,1,3,23]. Evidence suggests that the incidence of early-onset gastric cancer may be rising in the United States and other western countries [MS-2,11,12]. A recent global meta-analysis suggests factors contributing to early-onset gastric cancer risk include family history, H. pylori infection, and diet [MS-2,13].
epidemiology.demographics: Males have a higher incidence than females; the male-to-female ratio for diffuse type was 1.9 in US Whites [1]. HER2 positivity was significantly higher in males versus females [MS-13,139]. In the US population, the reported HER2 positivity rate in gastric cancer is 12% and is more often identified in the intestinal subtype (19%) rather than the diffuse subtype (6%) [MS-13,140]. Patients with diffuse type gastric cancer tend to be younger, whereas patients with intestinal type are more common among older adults [MS-13,1]. In contrast to high-income countries, tumors of the distal stomach continue to predominate in low and middle-income countries [MS-2,23]. Gastric cancer is one of the least commonly diagnosed cancers in Western Europe, sub-Saharan Africa, Australia, and North America [MS-2,6].

risk_factors.detail: H. pylori infection is a well-established risk factor for gastric cancer, particularly intestinal type [MS-2,2,3,9,14-16]. The guideline recommends testing for H. pylori infection and eradicating in all patients with early gastric cancer if positive [GAST-1,i]. If H. pylori testing is positive, recommendations should be discussed with family members as appropriate, and close family members should be tested [GAST-1,i]. H. pylori eradication for the prevention of metachronous gastric cancer has been supported by prospective trial data [64].

Tobacco smoking is associated with gastric cancer, particularly intestinal type [MS-2,2,3,9,14-16]. The guideline includes smoking cessation advice, counseling, and pharmacotherapy as indicated for all patients [GAST-1,g]. Smoking cessation is recommended as appropriate throughout survivorship [GAST-I 3 of 4].
High salt intake is associated with gastric cancer, particularly intestinal type [MS-2,2,3,9,14-16].
Various dietary factors are associated with gastric cancer risk, though specific factors beyond salt intake and H. pylori are not individually enumerated in this guideline [MS-2,2,3,9,14-16].

The role of alcohol as a risk factor for gastric cancer is without consensus. Meta-analyses have shown no appreciable association between light or moderate alcohol consumption and gastric cancer risk, but demonstrated a positive association between heavy alcohol use and gastric cancer, particularly non-cardia gastric cancer [MS-2,17-20].

It is estimated that 1% to 3% of gastric cancers are associated with inherited cancer predisposition syndromes, including hereditary diffuse gastric cancer, Lynch syndrome, juvenile polyposis syndrome, and familial adenomatous polyposis syndrome [MS-3]. Genetic risk assessment principles are outlined in GAST-D, referencing CDH1 pathogenic variant carriers and the NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment. Patients with MSI-H or dMMR tumors should be referred to a genetics counselor [GAST-B 5 of 7,13,160].

Age is a risk factor, with MSI-H/dMMR status associated with age ≥68 years [MS-14,116,119,150-153]. However, evidence suggests the incidence of early-onset gastric cancer may be rising in the US and other western countries [MS-2,11,12].
Males have higher incidence of gastric cancer than females [MS-2,1]. HER2 positivity was significantly higher in males versus females [MS-13,139].

The highest gastric cancer incidence rates occur in Northeast Asia, South and Central America, and Eastern Europe, with particularly high rates in Japan, Korea, and China [MS-2,5,6,9]. In the US, diffuse type gastric cancer incidence rate per 100,000 in US Whites was 4.4 [1]. Differences between US and Asian populations in HER2 testing and other biomarkers have been observed [MS-13].

protective_factors: H. pylori eradication therapy for prevention of metachronous gastric cancer [64, GAST-1, footnote i]
Improved food preservation and access to clean drinking water (associated with declining intestinal type gastric cancer rates in North America and Western Europe) [MS-2, 1, 3, 16, 23]
Diet with low promotion of gastric cancer [MS-2, 1, 16, 23]

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Gastric cancer is often diagnosed at an advanced stage in most countries where population screening is not routine. Approximately 50% of patients present with advanced disease at diagnosis and will likely have a poor outcome. The disease generally carries a poor prognosis because of late diagnosis. In Japan and South Korea, where population screening is performed widely, early detection often results in improved outcomes. In the United States, survival rates from gastric cancer remain poor as early detection continues to pose a major challenge for health care professionals. Over 95% of gastric cancers are adenocarcinomas, typically classified based on anatomic location (cardia/proximal or noncardia/distal) and histologic type (diffuse or intestinal). The diffuse type, characterized by poorly differentiated and discohesive tumor cells with signet-ring or non-signet-ring morphology, is more prevalent in low-risk areas and is mostly associated with heritable genetic abnormalities. The intestinal type, which forms a mass lesion with variably differentiated tumor cells arranged in a tubular or glandular pattern, occurs more frequently in high-risk areas. Intestinal type gastric cancer is often related to environmental factors such as H. pylori infection, tobacco smoking, high salt intake, and other dietary factors [1-3]. A dramatic shift in the type and location of upper gastrointestinal tract tumors has occurred in North America and Europe, with a marked decline in intestinal type gastric cancers of the distal stomach but rising incidence rates of diffuse type gastric cancer of the proximal stomach [1,3,21-23].

symptoms.name: Vague upper abdominal discomfort
symptoms.detail: Nonspecific upper abdominal symptoms that may be attributed to benign conditions, contributing to diagnostic delay

symptoms.name: Dyspepsia
symptoms.detail: Epigastric discomfort, early satiety, or postprandial fullness may be present

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss is a common presenting symptom, especially in advanced disease

symptoms.name: Anorexia
symptoms.detail: Loss of appetite may be an early or late symptom

symptoms.name: Dysphagia
symptoms.detail: Difficulty swallowing, particularly with proximal or EGJ tumors
symptoms.frequency: Variable, more common with proximal tumors

symptoms.name: Hematemesis or melena
symptoms.detail: Acute severe bleeding may occur, directly from the tumor or as a consequence of therapy; patients with acute severe bleeding should undergo prompt endoscopic assessment

signs.name: Alkaline phosphatase ≥100 U/L
signs.detail: An elevated alkaline phosphatase level ≥100 U/L is identified as a measure of poor outcome in gastric cancer

signs.name: Palpable abdominal mass
signs.detail: May be present in advanced locoregional or metastatic disease

signs.name: Virchow node
signs.detail: Left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy suggesting distant metastatic disease

atypical_or_missed: Early-stage gastric cancer may be asymptomatic or present with nonspecific dyspepsia that is attributed to benign conditions, leading to delayed diagnosis
In the United States and Western countries, the low incidence means screening programs are not practical, and diagnosis is often made late in the disease course

Young-onset gastric cancer may present differently; incidence of early-onset gastric cancer may be rising in the United States and other western countries, with factors including family history, H. pylori infection, and diet contributing to risk
Some gastric cancers present with occult gastrointestinal blood loss resulting in iron deficiency anemia

red_flags: Acute severe hematemesis or melena requiring prompt endoscopic assessment
Dysphagia suggesting proximal or EGJ involvement
Ascites on examination suggesting peritoneal disease
Virchow node (left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy) suggesting distant metastatic disease
Palpable left supraclavicular lymph node (suggests advanced disease)

Family history suggestive of hereditary diffuse gastric cancer syndrome (CDH1 pathogenic variant) or other cancer predisposition syndromes including Lynch syndrome, juvenile polyposis syndrome, and familial adenomatous polyposis syndrome

Evidence of locally advanced disease: infiltration of the root of the mesentery, para-aortic lymph node involvement highly suspicious on imaging or confirmed by biopsy, invasion or encasement of major vascular structures (excluding splenic vessels), or distant metastasis or peritoneal seeding including positive peritoneal cytology

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for diagnosis and staging; multiple biopsies (6–8) using standard-size endoscopy forceps should be performed to provide adequately sized material for histologic and molecular interpretation, especially in ulcerated lesions. Larger forceps may improve yield. The location of the tumor in the stomach and relative to the EGJ should be carefully recorded.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic type (intestinal vs diffuse), tumor grade, invasion depth, lymphovascular invasion (LVI)

diagnostic.test: Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) or endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD)
diagnostic.rationale: ER is essential for the accurate staging of early-stage cancers (T1a or T1b). EMR or ESD of focal nodules ≤2 cm can be safely performed to provide a larger specimen for pathologic assessment, providing greater information on degree of differentiation, LVI, and depth of infiltration, thereby providing accurate T-staging. Such excisional biopsies have the potential of being therapeutic.
diagnostic.key_findings: Accurate T-staging, depth of submucosal invasion in μm (if possible), status of lateral and deep margins, presence of LVI

diagnostic.rationale: Recommended if early-stage disease suspected or if early versus locally advanced disease needs to be determined. Important in initial clinical staging, providing evidence of depth of tumor invasion (T-category), presence of abnormal or enlarged lymph nodes (N-assessment), and occasionally signs of distant spread (M-category) or ascites. Hypoechoic expansion of gastric wall layers identifies tumor location. Accuracy of EUS: 57%-88% for T staging and 30%-90% for N staging. EUS is especially important in patients being considered for ER.
diagnostic.key_findings: T-category based on hypoechoic expansion of wall layers; N-assessment of perigastric lymph nodes; possible M-assessment (ascites, adjacent organ invasion)

diagnostic.rationale: Performed as clinically indicated to confirm metastatic disease and for biomarker testing

Laparoscopy with cytology is performed to evaluate for peritoneal spread when considering local therapy. Indicated for clinical stage T1b or higher. Not indicated if a palliative resection is planned. Positive peritoneal cytology (in the absence of visible peritoneal implants) is associated with poor prognosis and is defined as pM1 disease. In a study of 657 patients with potentially resectable gastric adenocarcinoma, metastatic disease (M1) was detected in 31% by laparoscopic staging.
diagnostic.key_findings: Peritoneal carcinomatosis, positive peritoneal cytology (defined as M1 disease), hepatic metastases, occult perigastric lymph nodes

diagnostic.rationale: Complete history and physical examination is part of the initial workup for all newly diagnosed patients
diagnostic.key_findings: Performance status, nutritional assessment, family history, signs of advanced disease

diagnostic.test: Complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive chemistry profile
diagnostic.rationale: Part of the standard initial workup; alkaline phosphatase level ≥100 U/L identified as a measure of poor outcome
diagnostic.key_findings: Anemia (possibly from chronic blood loss), elevated alkaline phosphatase, liver function abnormalities suggesting metastatic disease

staging.test: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with oral and IV contrast
staging.rationale: Part of routine preoperative staging; has an overall accuracy of 43% to 82% for measuring depth of invasion. CT with contrast should be considered if there were findings on the pre-treatment CT that merit more accurate anatomic assessment after treatment.
staging.indication: All newly diagnosed patients with gastric cancer

staging.rationale: Recommended for locally advanced or metastatic disease or if clinically indicated. May not be appropriate for T1 disease. Combined FDG-PET/CT imaging offers advantages over FDG-PET or CT alone, with a significantly higher accuracy rate in preoperative staging (68%) than FDG-PET (47%) or CT (53%) alone. FDG-PET has lower accuracy due to low FDG uptake in diffuse and mucinous tumor types. Significantly lower sensitivity compared to CT in detection of local lymph node involvement (56% vs 78%), though improved specificity (92% vs 62%). FDG-PET does not replace staging laparoscopy given inability to detect peritoneal disease.
staging.indication: Locally advanced or metastatic disease, or if clinically indicated

staging.test: Chest/abdomen CT with oral and IV contrast (response assessment)
staging.rationale: Performed after completion of perioperative systemic therapy and before surgical intervention for response assessment. Not required if FDG-PET/CT is done. Pelvis CT if clinically indicated.
Performed 5-8 weeks after therapy as clinically indicated for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors treated with neoadjuvant ICI
staging.indication: Response assessment after neoadjuvant immunotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR tumors

biomarkers.test: Universal testing for microsatellite instability (MSI) by PCR or NGS, or mismatch repair (MMR) by immunohistochemistry (IHC)
biomarkers.rationale: MSI-H or dMMR gastric tumors have different prognosis and treatment implications including response to immune checkpoint inhibitors. MSI-H/dMMR status is associated with earlier stage, ≥68 years of age, intestinal subtype, and distal stomach location. Testing performed on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue. Results interpreted as MSI-H or dMMR in accordance with CAP DNA Mismatch Repair Biomarker Reporting Guidelines. Testing should be performed only in CLIA-approved laboratories. Patients with MSI-H or dMMR tumors should be referred to genetics counselor.

biomarkers.test: Universal testing for PD-L1 by IHC
biomarkers.rationale: PD-L1 testing is used to identify patients who may benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. Combined positive score (CPS) ≥1 indicates PD-L1 expression. CPS and tumor area positivity (TAP) score have high concordance and may be interchangeable. A minimum of 100 tumor cells must be present for adequate evaluation. A companion diagnostic test should be used to analyze FFPE tumor tissue.
biomarkers.when: All newly diagnosed patients who are candidates for PD-1 or PD-L1 inhibitors

biomarkers.rationale: HER2 overexpression or amplification is a target for trastuzumab therapy. HER2 testing using IHC and, if needed, ISH/FISH is recommended. IHC scoring ranges from 0 to 3+. Score of 0 or 1+ = negative; 2+ = equivocal (requires ISH); 3+ = positive. Cases with ERBB2:CEP17 ratio ≥2 or average ERBB2 copy number ≥6 signals/cell are ISH positive. IHC/ISH/targeted gene PCR is the preferred initial approach.
biomarkers.when: If advanced/metastatic disease is documented or suspected

biomarkers.rationale: Claudin 18 isoform 2 (CLDN18.2) is a target for zolbetuximab therapy. Tumors considered CLDN18.2 positive if ≥75% of viable tumor cells demonstrate moderate to strong membrane CLDN18.2 staining (2+ or 3+ intensity) by IHC on FFPE tissue. CLDN18.2-positive tumors represent 24%-38% of advanced gastric and EGJ adenocarcinomas.
biomarkers.when: If advanced/metastatic disease is documented or suspected and zolbetuximab therapy is being considered

biomarkers.rationale: Offers the opportunity to assess numerous mutations simultaneously, along with amplification, deletions, TMB, and MSI status. NGS testing through a CLIA-approved laboratory may be considered later in the clinical course with sufficient tumor tissue. IHC/ISH/targeted gene PCR is the preferred initial approach.
biomarkers.when: Considered; not mandatory at initial diagnosis

biomarkers.rationale: When limited tissue is available or patients with metastatic/advanced gastric cancer cannot undergo traditional biopsy, multigene panel testing (MGPT) performed in a CLIA-approved laboratory may be considered. A negative result should be interpreted with caution as it does not exclude the presence of tumor.
biomarkers.when: When limited tissue available or traditional biopsy not feasible

biomarkers.rationale: Emerging potential biomarker for personalized treatment strategies; EBV testing should be performed if the morphology of the tumor contains prominent lymphoid stroma. Not currently recommended for clinical care routine testing.
biomarkers.when: If tumor morphology contains prominent lymphoid stroma

do_not_miss: Universal MSI/MMR testing in all newly diagnosed patients
Universal PD-L1 testing in all newly diagnosed patients who are candidates for PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors
HER2 testing if advanced/metastatic disease is documented or suspected
CLDN18.2 testing if advanced/metastatic disease is documented or suspected
Siewert classification for all adenocarcinomas involving the EGJ
H. pylori testing and eradication in all patients with early gastric cancer if positive
Screen for family history of gastric cancer
Assess for distress (NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List)
avoid: FDG-PET/CT may not be appropriate for T1 disease
EUS performed after chemotherapy or radiation therapy has reduced ability to accurately determine post-treatment stage
Biopsies performed after chemotherapy or radiation may not accurately diagnose the presence of residual disease but still provide useful information

staging.system: American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) 8th Edition (2017) TNM Staging Classification for Carcinoma of the Stomach. This system includes clinical staging (cTNM), pathologic staging (pTNM), and post-neoadjuvant pathologic staging (ypTNM). Tumors involving the EGJ with an epicenter located >2 cm into the proximal stomach are staged as gastric carcinomas. Tumors involving the EGJ with an epicenter ≤2 cm into the proximal stomach are staged as esophageal carcinomas. Cancers within the gastric cardia not involving the EGJ are staged as gastric carcinomas.

t_stages.description: Carcinoma in situ: intraepithelial tumor without invasion of the lamina propria, high-grade dysplasia
Tumor invades the lamina propria, muscularis mucosae, or submucosa
Tumor invades the lamina propria or muscularis mucosae
Tumor invades the submucosa. Superficial pT1b is defined as ≤500 μm invasion of submucosa; deep pT1b is defined as >500 μm invasion of submucosa.

Tumor invades the muscularis propria. A tumor may penetrate the muscularis propria with extension into the gastrocolic or gastrohepatic ligaments, or into the greater or lesser omentum, without perforation of the visceral peritoneum covering these structures; in this case, the tumor is classified as T3.
Tumor penetrates the subserosal connective tissue without invasion of the visceral peritoneum or adjacent structures
Tumor invades the serosa (visceral peritoneum) or adjacent structures
Tumor invades the serosa (visceral peritoneum). If there is perforation of the visceral peritoneum covering the gastric ligaments or the omentum, the tumor should be classified as T4a.

Tumor invades adjacent structures/organs. The adjacent structures of the stomach include the spleen, transverse colon, liver, diaphragm, pancreas, abdominal wall, adrenal gland, kidney, small intestine, and retroperitoneum.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph node(s) cannot be assessed
Metastasis in 1 to 2 regional lymph nodes
Metastasis in 3 to 6 regional lymph nodes
Metastasis in 7 or more regional lymph nodes
Metastasis in 7 to 15 regional lymph nodes
Metastasis in 16 or more regional lymph nodes

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis. Positive peritoneal cytology (in the absence of visible peritoneal implants) is defined as pM1 disease.

stage_groups.criteria: cTis, cN0, cM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ confined to epithelium without lamina propria invasion

stage_groups.criteria: cT1, cN0, cM0 or cT2, cN0, cM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Early-stage tumor confined to gastric wall with no nodal involvement

stage_groups.criteria: cT1, cN1-N3, cM0; cT2, cN1-N3, cM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locoregional disease with varying T and N involvement

stage_groups.criteria: cT3, cN0, cM0; cT4a, cN0, cM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locoregional disease without nodal involvement but higher T stage

stage_groups.criteria: cT3, cN1-N3, cM0; cT4a, cN1-N3, cM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced disease with significant nodal involvement

stage_groups.criteria: cT4b, any cN, cM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invading adjacent structures
stage_groups.treatment_intent: May be curative in selected cases

stage_groups.criteria: Any cT, any cN, cM1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastatic disease

stage_groups.criteria: pTis, pN0, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ, no nodal or distant metastasis

stage_groups.criteria: pT1, pN0, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor confined to mucosa or submucosa without nodal involvement

stage_groups.criteria: pT1, pN1, pM0; pT2, pN0, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Early tumor with minimal nodal involvement or deeper invasion without nodes

stage_groups.criteria: pT1, pN2, pM0; pT2, pN1, pM0; pT3, pN0, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Intermediate-stage disease

stage_groups.criteria: pT1, pN3a, pM0; pT2, pN2, pM0; pT3, pN1, pM0; pT4a, pN0, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced disease with moderate nodal burden

stage_groups.criteria: pT2, pN3a, pM0; pT3, pN2, pM0; pT4a, pN1 or pN2, pM0; pT4b, pN0, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced locoregional disease

stage_groups.criteria: pT1, pN3b, pM0; pT2, pN3b, pM0; pT3, pN3a, pM0; pT4a, pN3a, pM0; pT4b, pN1 or pN2, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced locoregional disease with high nodal burden

stage_groups.criteria: pT3, pN3b, pM0; pT4a, pN3b, pM0; pT4b, pN3a or pN3b, pM0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very advanced locoregional disease

stage_groups.criteria: Any pT, any pN, pM1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastatic disease

stage_groups.criteria: ypT1, ypN0, M0; ypT2, ypN0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Response to neoadjuvant therapy with minimal residual disease

stage_groups.criteria: ypT3, ypN0, M0; ypT2, ypN1, M0; ypT1, ypN2, M0; ypT4a, ypN0, M0; ypT3, ypN1, M0; ypT2, ypN2, M0; ypT1, ypN3, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Moderate residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy

stage_groups.criteria: ypT4a, ypN1, M0; ypT3, ypN2, M0; ypT2, ypN3, M0; ypT4b, ypN0, M0; ypT4b, ypN1, M0; ypT4a, ypN2, M0; ypT3, ypN3, M0; ypT4b, ypN2, M0; ypT4b, ypN3, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Significant residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy

stage_groups.criteria: Any ypT, any ypN, M1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastatic disease after neoadjuvant therapy

staging_pearls: Intramural extension to the duodenum or esophagus is not considered invasion of an adjacent structure but is classified using the depth of the greatest invasion in any of these sites
If there is perforation of the visceral peritoneum covering the gastric ligaments or the omentum, the tumor should be classified as T4a
Positive peritoneal cytology (in the absence of visible peritoneal implants) is defined as M1 disease

A tumor penetrating the muscularis propria with extension into the gastrocolic or gastrohepatic ligaments, or into the greater or lesser omentum, without perforation of the visceral peritoneum covering these structures, is classified as T3
The yp prefix is used to indicate cases in which staging is performed following preoperative therapy
Superficial pT1b is defined as ≤500 μm invasion of submucosa and deep pT1b is defined as >500 μm invasion of submucosa
The staging system was validated using the International Gastric Cancer Association (IGCA) database of >25,000 patients with gastric cancer
New cTNM and ypTNM stage groupings were added in the 8th edition to overcome the lack of validated clinical staging and post-neoadjuvant staging in the 7th edition
EUS is not reliable for predicting superficial or deep submucosal invasion, so endoscopic resection is recommended to provide accurate staging
Pathologic regression after neoadjuvant therapy is assessed using a 4-tiered scoring system: 0 (complete response), 1 (near complete), 2 (partial response), 3 (poor or no response)

management_principles.overview: The NCCN Guidelines emphasize a multidisciplinary team (MDT) approach as essential for the treatment of localized gastric cancer. Treatment philosophy integrates surgery, systemic therapy, and radiation therapy based on disease stage, biomarker status, and patient fitness. Best supportive care is an integral part of treatment, especially in patients with unresectable, recurrent, or metastatic disease. The primary goal for localized disease is curative resection with negative margins, often combined with perioperative systemic therapy. For advanced disease, systemic therapy aims to prolong survival and improve quality of life. Clinical trial participation is strongly encouraged throughout the disease course. The panel believes that combined modality therapy for localized esophagogastric cancer is optimally delivered with an infrastructure that encourages multidisciplinary treatment decision-making by members of all relevant disciplines taking care of these patients.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with localized disease (cTis, cT1a, or cT1b-high to cT4a, N any, M0) who are medically fit and potentially resectable.
by_intent.approach: Multimodal therapy with the primary intent of achieving cure. For early-stage (cTis or cT1a) tumors, endoscopic resection (ER) may be sufficient. For resectable T1b or higher tumors, surgery is the primary treatment, with perioperative systemic therapy (category 1) recommended for cT2 or higher, any N. For MSI-H/dMMR tumors, neoadjuvant or perioperative immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) therapy may be considered. Postoperative management is dictated by pathologic stage and extent of lymph node dissection.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with unresectable locoregional disease, metastatic disease (cM1), or those who are nonsurgical candidates.
by_intent.approach: Treatment focuses on symptom control, prolonging survival, and improving quality of life. Options include systemic therapy, chemoradiation (for locally unresectable disease not previously treated), and best supportive care. The selection of therapy is heavily dependent on performance status, biomarker status (HER2, PD-L1, CLDN18.2, MSI/MMR), and prior treatment. A multimodality interdisciplinary approach to palliative care is encouraged.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: The NCCN Panel believes that comprehensive care for gastric cancer requires expertise in several disciplines, including surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, gastroenterology, radiology, and pathology. The presence of nutritional services, social workers, nurses, palliative care specialists, and other supporting disciplines is also desirable. The infrastructure should encourage multidisciplinary treatment decision-making with joint review of patient data. Frequent meetings (once a week or every 2 weeks) are encouraged. All relevant disciplines should be encouraged to participate. Long-term therapeutic strategies should be developed after adequate staging procedures are completed but ideally prior to any therapy. A brief documentation of the consensus recommendation(s) by the MDT may be useful. Recommendations made by the MDT may be considered advisory to the primary treating physicians. Re-presentation of select patient outcomes after therapy is an effective educational method.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is a critical factor in treatment decision-making. The Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group Performance Status Scale (ECOG PS) and the Karnofsky Performance Status Scale (KPS) are commonly used. Patients with a KPS score <60% or an ECOG PS score ≥3 should be offered palliative/best supportive care only. Systemic therapy or chemoradiation (only if locally unresectable and not previously received) can be offered in addition to palliative/best supportive care for patients with better performance status (KPS score of ≥60% or ECOG PS score ≤2). The choice of systemic therapy regimens is dependent on performance status and medical comorbidities. Two-drug cytotoxic regimens are preferred for patients with advanced disease due to lower toxicity. The use of three cytotoxic drugs should be reserved for patients who are medically fit with excellent PS and easy access to frequent toxicity evaluations.

management_pathways.title: Localized Disease: cTis or cT1a or Superficial cT1b

options.label: Endoscopic Resection (ER)
options.preference: Preferred for staging and potential cure
options.recommendation: ER (EMR or ESD) is essential for accurate staging of early-stage cancers and can be therapeutic. Curative features include small size (≤2 cm), superficial invasion (T1a or T1b ≤500 µm), well or moderately differentiated, complete excision with negative margins.

options.label: Surgery
options.preference: Appropriate for ≥T1b cancer or actively bleeding cancer, or when postoperative therapy is preferred
options.recommendation: Gastrectomy with lymphadenectomy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: For pTis or pT1a, N0 (including superficial pT1b) following R0 resection, observation is recommended due to relatively good prognosis and lack of evidence for benefit from postoperative therapy.

options.label: Endoscopic Resection (ER)
options.recommendation: ER is recommended for nonsurgical candidates with cTis or cT1a tumors.

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Surgery as primary therapy is appropriate for ≥T1b cancer.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Postoperative management depends on final pathologic stage and margin status (see GAST-4).

management_pathways.title: Locoregional Disease (cM0, Any N): Medically Fit, Potentially Resectable

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Surgery as primary therapy is appropriate.

options.label: Perioperative Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Perioperative systemic therapy is a category 1 recommendation for localized gastric cancer. Preferred regimens include FLOT (category 1) or fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin.

options.label: Consider Neoadjuvant/Perioperative ICI
options.recommendation: Consider neoadjuvant or perioperative immune checkpoint inhibitor(s) (ICI) if tumor is MSI-H/dMMR. Multidisciplinary evaluation is recommended.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Response assessment is performed after neoadjuvant/perioperative therapy. Management is then based on surgical outcomes (GAST-4/GAST-5).

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Surgery as primary therapy is appropriate.

options.label: Perioperative Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Perioperative systemic therapy is an option.

options.label: Consider Neoadjuvant/Perioperative ICI
options.recommendation: In patients with a MSI-H/dMMR tumor, perioperative immunotherapy should be considered in consultation with a multidisciplinary team. Gastrectomy with appropriate lymphadenectomy remains the standard approach outside of prospective organ-preservation clinical trials.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Response assessment includes FDG-PET/CT (5–8 weeks after therapy) as clinically indicated, EGD and biopsy. If radiologic and endoscopic complete response occurs, gastrectomy remains the standard outside of organ-preservation trials. Immunotherapy treatment should be given for at least 1 year (counting perioperative and adjuvant settings).

management_pathways.title: Locoregional Disease (cM0, Any N): Medically Fit, Surgically Unresectable

options.label: Chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Chemoradiation or systemic therapy are the recommended treatment options.

options.label: Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Chemoradiation or systemic therapy are the recommended treatment options.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After primary treatment, restaging determines if surgery becomes an option.

management_pathways.title: Nonsurgical Candidates with Locoregional Disease (cM0, Any N)

nodes.criteria: Medically unable to tolerate major surgery or patients who are medically fit, but decline surgery

options.label: Palliative Management
options.recommendation: Nonsurgical candidates with locoregional disease should receive palliative management/best supportive care.

management_pathways.title: Unresectable Locally Advanced, Recurrent, or Metastatic Disease: First-Line Therapy

options.label: Trastuzumab + Chemotherapy ± Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and trastuzumab. Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, trastuzumab, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and trastuzumab (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, trastuzumab, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1).

options.label: ICI + Chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and nivolumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for PD-L1 CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for PD-L1 CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for PD-L1 CPS ≥5).

options.label: Zolbetuximab + Chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and zolbetuximab-clzb for CLDN18.2 positive (category 1).

options.label: ICI Monotherapy or Combination
options.recommendation: Pembrolizumab, Dostarlimab-gxly, Nivolumab and ipilimumab, Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and nivolumab, Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and pembrolizumab.

management_pathways.title: Unresectable Locally Advanced, Recurrent, or Metastatic Disease: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy
branching_basis: Biomarker status (MSI-H, TMB-H, NTRK, BRAF, RET)

options.label: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki
options.recommendation: For HER2-positive adenocarcinoma following progression on prior trastuzumab-based regimen.

options.label: Various Chemotherapy/Targeted Options
options.recommendation: Ramucirumab and paclitaxel (category 1), Docetaxel (category 1), Paclitaxel (category 1), Irinotecan (category 1), Fluorouracil and irinotecan, Trifluridine and tipiracil for third-line or subsequent therapy (category 1).

options.label: Pembrolizumab, Dostarlimab-gxly, or Nivolumab and ipilimumab
options.recommendation: If no prior checkpoint inhibitor therapy or no tumor progression while on therapy with a checkpoint inhibitor.

options.label: TRK Inhibitors
options.recommendation: Entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib. Repotrectinib can be used in patients whose disease progressed on a prior TRK-targeted therapy.

nodes.criteria: Low PCI (≤10), stable or improved disease, no extraperitoneal metastases

options.label: Cytoreductive Surgery + IC/HIPEC
options.recommendation: Gastrectomy with cytoreductive surgery and intraperitoneal chemotherapy (IC)/hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) can be used in conjunction with cytoreductive surgery for patients with low PCI (≤10) who are candidates to undergo complete cytoreduction. Decision should be made after multidisciplinary discussion.

options.label: Continue Systemic Therapy or Clinical Trial
options.recommendation: If incomplete cytoreduction predicted, or as alternative.

nodes.criteria: High PCI (>10), progression of disease, or extraperitoneal metastases
Locoregional recurrence, resectable and medically operable

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Review if surgery is appropriate for patients with isolated local recurrences. Surgery should be considered as an option for locoregional recurrence in patients who are medically fit.

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence, unresectable or medically inoperable

options.recommendation: Palliative Management.

options.recommendation: Palliative Management.

items.recommendation: Required for all patients.
Recommended for all patients.

items.recommendation: Recommended for all patients.

items.text: Screen for family history of gastric cancers
items.recommendation: Recommended. See Principles of Genetic Risk Assessment for Gastric Cancer (GAST-D).

items.text: Test for Helicobacter pylori infection and eradicate in all patients with early gastric cancer if positive
items.recommendation: Recommended. If testing is positive, discuss recommendations with family members as appropriate.
Recommended. Refer to the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List (DIS-A).
Required. Multiple (6–8) biopsies should be performed. Location of tumor should be carefully recorded.

items.text: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with oral and IV contrast
items.recommendation: Required for staging.
Recommended for locally advanced or metastatic disease or if clinically indicated. May not be appropriate for T1 disease.

Recommended if early-stage disease suspected or if early versus locally advanced disease needs to be determined (preferred). EUS is not reliable for predicting superficial or deep submucosal invasion.
Essential for accurate staging of early-stage cancers (T1a or T1b). Early-stage cancers can best be diagnosed by ER. ER may also be therapeutic for early-stage disease.

Recommended to evaluate for peritoneal spread when considering local therapy for patients who are medically fit with stage cT1b or higher potentially resectable locoregional disease. Not indicated if a palliative resection is planned.
As clinically indicated.

items.text: Universal testing for microsatellite instability (MSI) by PCR/NGS or mismatch repair (MMR) by immunohistochemistry (IHC)
items.recommendation: Recommended in all newly diagnosed patients. Testing should be performed in CLIA-approved laboratories.

items.text: Universal testing for programmed death ligand 1 (PD-L1) by IHC
items.recommendation: Recommended in all newly diagnosed patients. A companion diagnostic test should be used to analyze FFPE tumor tissue. Testing should be performed in CLIA-approved laboratories.
Recommended if advanced/metastatic disease is documented/suspected. Assessment for tumor HER2 overexpression using IHC and ISH/FISH is recommended.

Recommended if advanced/metastatic disease is documented/suspected and zolbetuximab therapy is being considered. Tumors are positive if ≥75% viable tumor cells demonstrate moderate to strong membrane CLDN18.2 staining (2+ or 3+ intensity).
Should be considered. IHC/ISH/targeted gene PCR is the preferred initial approach. NGS through a CLIA-approved laboratory may be considered later in the clinical course with sufficient tumor tissue.

When limited tissue is available or for patients not able to undergo traditional biopsy, multigene panel testing (MGPT) on ctDNA performed in a CLIA-approved laboratory may be considered. A negative result should be interpreted with caution.
For all patients with adenocarcinomas involving the esophagogastric junction (EGJ).

Recommended prior to initiating treatment. The team should include surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, gastroenterology, radiology, pathology, nutrition, social work, and palliative care as needed.
Recommended for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors in the appropriate clinical context.

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary treatment option for patients with localized gastric cancer with the goal of achieving a complete resection with negative margins (R0 resection).
principles: The primary goal is R0 resection; however, only 50% of patients will achieve this.
The type of resection (subtotal vs. total gastrectomy) and extent of lymph node dissection (D1 vs. D2) are key considerations.
Adequate gastric resection with lymphadenectomy is standard for T1b–T3 tumors. T4b tumors require en bloc resection of involved structures.
Gastric resection should include regional lymphatics with a goal of examining ≥16 lymph nodes (retrieval and assessment of >30 nodes is desirable).
Routine splenectomy is not indicated unless the spleen is involved or extensive hilar adenopathy is noted.
Surgery for palliation should be reserved for symptoms (obstruction, uncontrollable bleeding) when nonsurgical options are not feasible.

procedures.name: Distal Gastrectomy
procedures.indicated_for: Tumors in the distal two-thirds of the stomach.
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, dumping syndrome, nutritional deficiencies.

procedures.name: Subtotal Gastrectomy
procedures.indicated_for: Selected distal gastric cancers. Has similar outcomes to total gastrectomy for distal cancers with fewer complications.

procedures.name: Total Gastrectomy
procedures.indicated_for: Proximal gastric cancers, diffuse-type cancer, or when adequate margins require it.
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, dumping syndrome, severe nutritional deficiencies (B12, iron, etc.), weight loss.

procedures.name: Endoscopic Resection (EMR or ESD)
procedures.indicated_for: Selected early-stage (T1a or superficial T1b ≤500 µm) gastric cancers meeting specific criteria (≤2 cm, well/moderately differentiated, no LVI, negative margins).

procedures.name: Cytoreductive Surgery (CRS) with HIPEC
procedures.approach: Open surgery followed by hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy
procedures.indicated_for: Carefully selected patients with peritoneal carcinoma as only disease, low PCI (≤10), and potential for complete cytoreduction.
procedures.key_complications: Bowel anastomotic leak, intra-abdominal abscess, renal dysfunction.
procedures.operative_mortality: Variable, reported as 0% in some series.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Gastric adenocarcinoma is considered unresectable for cure if there is evidence of: 1) Locoregionally advanced disease: infiltration of the root of the mesentery or para-aortic lymph nodes highly suspicious on imaging or confirmed by biopsy; invasion or encasement of major vascular structures (excluding the splenic vessels); 2) Distant metastasis or peritoneal seeding (including positive peritoneal cytology, which is defined as pM1 disease).
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Consider placing a feeding tube in select patients undergoing total gastrectomy, especially if postoperative chemoradiation appears likely. Nutritional assessment and optimization are important. For patients undergoing non-operative management (e.g., immunotherapy for MSI-H/dMMR), gastrectomy with appropriate lymphadenectomy remains the standard outside of prospective organ-preservation clinical trials.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is primarily used in the postoperative adjuvant setting for patients who received less than a D2 lymph node dissection, and for palliation of symptoms (bleeding, pain, dysphagia) in patients with advanced disease. It is also a component of definitive chemoradiation for patients with locally advanced, surgically unresectable disease who are not candidates for surgery.
principles: Treatment recommendations should be made after joint consultation and discussion by a multidisciplinary team.
All available information from pretreatment diagnostic studies (CT, EUS, EGD, FDG-PET/CT) should be used to determine the target volume.
CT simulation and conformal treatment planning (3D-CRT or IMRT) should be used.
Image guidance may be used to enhance clinical targeting.

In general, Siewert Type I and II tumors should be managed with RT guidelines applicable to esophageal and EGJ cancers. Siewert Type III tumors may be managed with either esophageal/EGJ or gastric cancer RT guidelines depending on tumor bulk.
Treatment interruptions or dose reductions for manageable acute toxicities should be avoided. Careful patient monitoring and aggressive supportive care are preferable.

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant Postoperative Chemoradiation
dose_frameworks.indication: Patients with R0 or R1 resection for gastric or EGJ adenocarcinoma who received less than a D2 lymph node dissection. Recommended for all patients following an R1 resection. Also an option for patients with pT2, N0 tumors with high-risk features.

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive Chemoradiation for Unresectable Disease
dose_frameworks.indication: Patients with locally advanced, surgically unresectable disease who are not candidates for surgery.

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative Radiation
dose_frameworks.indication: Symptom control for bleeding, pain, or dysphagia in patients with advanced disease.

approaches.name: Adjuvant Chemoradiation with Concurrent Fluoropyrimidine
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Fluoropyrimidine (infusional fluorouracil or capecitabine). Leucovorin is indicated with certain fluorouracil-based regimens.
approaches.indication: Postoperative adjuvant therapy after less than D2 dissection (category 1).
approaches.key_trial: INT-0116 (Macdonald et al., N Engl J Med 2001).
approaches.toxicities: Grade 3–4 hematologic (54%) and GI (33%) toxicities were reported in the original trial. Nausea, diarrhea, and hematologic toxicity are common.

approaches.name: Definitive Chemoradiation with Fluoropyrimidine ± Platinum
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Preferred: Fluoropyrimidine, Fluorouracil and oxaliplatin, Fluorouracil and cisplatin. Other: Fluoropyrimidine and paclitaxel (category 2B).
approaches.indication: Unresectable locally advanced disease if not previously received.
approaches.key_trial: PRODIGE5/ACCORD17 (Conroy et al., Lancet Oncol 2014) for FOLFOX vs fluorouracil/cisplatin in esophageal cancer (extrapolated).

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is a cornerstone of treatment for both localized (perioperative) and advanced/metastatic gastric cancer. Regimens are chosen based on disease setting, performance status, comorbidities, and biomarker status (HER2, PD-L1, MSI/MMR, CLDN18.2). Two-drug cytotoxic regimens are preferred for advanced disease due to lower toxicity. The use of three cytotoxic drugs should be reserved for medically fit patients with excellent performance status. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for any recommended systemic biologic therapy.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: FLOT (Fluorouracil, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, docetaxel) (category 1). FLOT + durvalumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 or TAP ≥1% (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine and oxaliplatin.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: The FLOT4 trial demonstrated superior OS with perioperative FLOT vs. ECF. The MATTERHORN trial showed improved EFS with the addition of durvalumab to FLOT in PD-L1 positive tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: For FLOT + durvalumab, PD-L1 CPS ≥1 or TAP ≥1% is preferred. It is also recommended for clinical node-negative tumors (category 2A). Use for diffuse type tumors showed no OS advantage (category 2B).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Dostarlimab-gxly for neoadjuvant therapy only. Nivolumab and ipilimumab followed by nivolumab. Pembrolizumab. Tremelimumab and durvalumab for neoadjuvant therapy only.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: High pathologic CR rates observed in trials. Gastrectomy remains the standard outside of organ-preservation trials.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and trastuzumab. Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, trastuzumab, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and trastuzumab (category 1). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, trastuzumab, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on ToGA and KEYNOTE-811 trials.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+ and ISH/FISH+).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and nivolumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on CheckMate-649, KEYNOTE-859, and RATIONALE-305 trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and pembrolizumab for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5). Fluoropyrimidine, cisplatin, and tislelizumab-jsgr for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (category 1 for CPS ≥5).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and zolbetuximab-clzb for CLDN18.2 positive (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on SPOTLIGHT and GLOW trials.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HER2-negative, CLDN18.2-positive (≥75% tumor cells with moderate to strong membranous staining).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Metastatic Disease (MSI-H/dMMR Tumors, Independent of PD-L1)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Pembrolizumab. Dostarlimab-gxly. Nivolumab and ipilimumab. Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and nivolumab. Fluoropyrimidine, oxaliplatin, and pembrolizumab.

Ramucirumab and paclitaxel (category 1). Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki for HER2-positive adenocarcinoma (category 1). Docetaxel (category 1). Paclitaxel (category 1). Irinotecan (category 1). Fluorouracil and irinotecan. Trifluridine and tipiracil for third-line or subsequent therapy (category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on RAINBOW, DESTINY-Gastric01/02, COUGAR-02, WJOG 4007, and TAGS trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ramucirumab (category 1), Irinotecan and cisplatin, Fluorouracil and irinotecan + ramucirumab, Irinotecan and ramucirumab, Docetaxel and irinotecan (category 2B).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None for general options. Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki requires HER2 positivity.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Entrectinib, larotrectinib, or repotrectinib for NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusion-positive tumors. Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR tumors. Nivolumab and ipilimumab for MSI-H/dMMR tumors. Pembrolizumab for TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) tumors. Dostarlimab-gxly for MSI-H/dMMR tumors. Dabrafenib and trametinib for BRAF V600E-mutated tumors. Selpercatinib for RET gene fusion-positive tumors.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Tumor-agnostic approvals based on basket trials.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Specific molecular alterations (NTRK fusion, MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, BRAF V600E, RET fusion).
